question: HOLIDAY / HOLIDAY PAY (12)

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement
Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, workers are entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks' paid holiday per year, which includes full-time, part-time, and zero-hours contract workers. This statutory entitlement amounts to 28 days for someone working a five-day week, and part-time workers receive a proportional amount based on the hours they work. Holiday entitlement starts accruing from the first day of employment and continues during periods such as probation, sickness, and statutory leave (e.g., maternity or paternity leave). Employers may offer enhanced holiday entitlements beyond the statutory minimum, which should be detailed in the employment contract.

Holiday pay must be calculated fairly and cannot be included as a part of the hourly rate (known as "rolled-up holiday pay") for most workers, except in some cases for irregular-hours or part-year workers if their leave year started before 31 March 2024. When an employee leaves a job, their final pay may include payment for any unused holiday entitlement, and employers should clearly explain how the final pay is calculated, including any deductions or additional payments.

Step 1: Check your employment contract for your holiday entitlement and whether it includes any enhanced holiday beyond the statutory minimum.  
Step 2: Keep track of the holiday you take and any accrued but unused holiday.  
Step 3: If you leave your job, ensure you receive payment for any unused holiday as part of your final pay and ask your employer for a clear breakdown if needed.  
Step 4: Contact ACAS or Citizens Advice if you have questions or disputes about your holiday entitlement or pay.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job

question: My employer has not paid my holiday pay. What can I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has not paid you for holiday days you have taken, you may be entitled to receive holiday pay. Holiday pay is the money you should get for the time you take off work as annual leave. You should first check your employment contract and any company policies to confirm your entitlement to holiday pay. If you believe you are owed holiday pay, you should raise the issue with your employer and ask them to pay what they owe you.

If talking to your employer does not resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance with your employer about the unpaid holiday pay. If this still does not lead to payment, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unpaid wages. It is important to keep any evidence that supports your claim, such as your contract, payslips, and records of the holiday you took.

Step 1: Check your contract and company policies for holiday pay entitlement.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer and explain the issue, providing any evidence you have.  
Step 3: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance with your employer.  
Step 4: Consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about taking further action.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: How is holiday pay calculated if I work irregular hours?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you work irregular hours, your holiday pay is calculated based on your average pay over a relevant period, usually the last 52 weeks in which you were paid. To work this out, your employer will add up all the pay you received for the hours you worked during those weeks and then divide by the number of weeks worked to find an average weekly pay. This average is then used to calculate the pay you should receive for your holiday entitlement. For irregular-hours workers, all holiday leave must be paid at your ‘normal’ rate of pay, which includes regular overtime and commission but usually excludes bonuses.

If you have worked less than 52 weeks, the calculation will be based on the weeks you have worked. Your employer should use this average pay to ensure you receive the correct holiday pay for the time off you take.

Step 1: Your employer calculates your average weekly pay based on the last 52 weeks you were paid (or fewer if you have not worked that long).  
Step 2: They multiply this average weekly pay by the number of weeks of holiday you take.  
Step 3: You receive holiday pay at this calculated average ‘normal’ rate.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: Can my employer include holiday pay in my hourly rate?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers generally cannot include holiday pay in your hourly rate, a practice known as ‘rolled-up holiday pay’. This means your holiday pay should be paid separately from your normal wages. However, there is an exception for workers with irregular hours or part-year workers: employers can use rolled-up holiday pay for these workers unless their leave year began on or before 31 March 2024.

If you work irregular hours or part-year and your leave year started after 31 March 2024, your holiday pay should be calculated separately and not included in your hourly rate. For more detailed guidance or questions about your specific situation, you can contact Acas.

Step 1: Check if you are a worker with irregular hours or part-year employment.  
Step 2: Confirm when your leave year began (before or after 31 March 2024).  
Step 3: If your employer includes holiday pay in your hourly rate and this does not apply to your situation, you may want to seek further advice from Acas.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights

question: I have taken annual leave but did not get paid for it. What are my rights?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you have taken annual leave but were not paid for it, you are generally entitled to receive holiday pay for the time you were on leave. Holiday pay is a legal right, and your employer should pay you for the holidays you have taken according to your contract and the law. If your employer has not paid you for your annual leave, you should first check your employment contract and any company policies to confirm your entitlement to holiday pay.

If you believe you are owed holiday pay, you should gather any evidence such as your contract, payslips, and records of the leave you took. Then, raise the issue with your employer or payroll department to request the payment. If the employer refuses or does not respond, you may consider seeking advice from HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) or an employment adviser.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and company policies for holiday pay terms.  
Step 2: Collect evidence of your leave and unpaid holiday pay.  
Step 3: Contact your employer or payroll department to request payment.  
Step 4: If unresolved, seek advice from HMRC or an employment advisory service.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: How much holiday am I legally entitled to in the UK?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, workers are legally entitled to a minimum of 5.6 weeks' paid holiday per year. This applies whether you work full-time, part-time, or under a zero-hours contract. For example, if you work five days a week, this equates to 28 days of paid holiday, which can include bank holidays if your contract states so. Part-time workers receive holiday entitlement proportional to the hours they work, ensuring they are not treated less favourably than full-time workers.

If you have been employed for less than a full year, you are entitled to a proportion of the full holiday entitlement based on how long you have worked. Holiday entitlement starts accruing from your first day of work, including during periods such as probation, sick leave, or statutory leave like maternity or paternity leave. Some employers may offer enhanced or contractual holiday entitlement above the legal minimum, so it is important to check your employment contract.

Step 1: Check your employment contract or written statement of employment particulars to see your holiday entitlement and whether bank holidays are included.  
Step 2: If unsure, speak to your manager, HR department, or employer for clarification.  
Step 3: Use the GOV.UK holiday calculator to estimate your entitlement based on your working pattern.  
Step 4: Contact the Acas helpline if you have further questions or concerns about your holiday entitlement.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement

question: Does holiday continue to build up while I am off sick?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, holiday entitlement continues to build up while you are off work sick. This means that even if you are on sick leave, you still accumulate your statutory holiday entitlement. Employers must allow you to take this accrued holiday either during or after your period of sickness absence.

If you have any concerns about your holiday entitlement or pay while off sick, you can discuss this with your employer or contact the Acas helpline for guidance.

Step 1: Check your employment contract or company policy on holiday and sick leave.
Step 2: Keep records of your sick leave and holiday entitlement.
Step 3: If there is a dispute, raise it with your employer or seek advice from Acas.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave

question: What happens to my unused holiday when I leave my job?

sources.title: Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

When you leave your job, you are entitled to be paid for any unused holiday entitlement that you have accrued but not taken. This payment is usually included in your final pay. Your employer should calculate how much holiday pay you are owed based on your usual pay and the amount of holiday you have left. The final pay may also include other elements like redundancy pay or deductions for training courses, but the holiday pay should be clearly shown on your payslip.

Your final pay should be given to you on your normal pay date, even if you leave partway through a pay period. If you do not receive a payslip explaining your final pay or if you have questions about it, you should speak to your employer or contact the Acas helpline for guidance.

Step 1: Check your contract or employee handbook for details on holiday entitlement and final pay.  
Step 2: When leaving, ask your employer how your unused holiday pay has been calculated and request a payslip showing this.  
Step 3: If you disagree or have not received your final pay, raise the issue with your employer or seek advice from Acas or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job

question: Can my employer refuse my holiday request?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can refuse your holiday request, but they must have a good reason for doing so. Workers have the right to request holiday, but employers may refuse if the timing would cause problems for the business. However, paid annual leave is a legal right, so your employer must still provide you with your holiday entitlement, even if they refuse a specific request. If you disagree with their decision, you can try to resolve the dispute through discussion or by seeking advice from organisations like ACAS.

Step 1: Submit your holiday request in writing, giving your employer enough notice.  
Step 2: If your employer refuses, ask for the reason and try to discuss alternative dates.  
Step 3: If you cannot agree, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for help resolving the issue.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: Can my employer force me to take holiday?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, your employer can require you to take holiday at certain times, but they must give you notice at least twice as long as the length of the holiday they want you to take. For example, if they want you to take one week off, they must give you at least two weeks' notice. This is to ensure you have enough time to plan and prepare. Holiday entitlement is a legal right, and your employer must provide it, but they can decide when you take it, provided they follow the correct notice rules.

If you disagree with the timing of the holiday your employer has set, you should try to discuss it with them to find a suitable arrangement. If you think your rights to holiday or holiday pay are not being met, there are ways to resolve disputes, including contacting ACAS for advice.

Step 1: Check your employment contract for any specific holiday rules.  
Step 2: If your employer wants you to take holiday, ensure they give you the correct notice (twice the length of the holiday).  
Step 3: Discuss any concerns with your employer.  
Step 4: If unresolved, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: Do part-time workers get holiday pay?

sources.title: How much holiday someone gets – Holiday entitlement

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, part-time workers in the UK are entitled to holiday pay. By law, all workers, including part-time employees, have the right to 5.6 weeks of statutory paid holiday per year. The amount of holiday pay for part-time workers is proportional to the hours they work, ensuring they are not treated less favourably than full-time workers. If an employer offers more than the statutory minimum holiday entitlement to full-time workers, they must also provide more than the minimum to part-time workers.

Holiday entitlement starts accruing from the first day of work, including during probation, sick leave, or statutory leave such as maternity or paternity leave. Part-time workers should check their employment contract to understand if they have any enhanced or additional holiday rights beyond the statutory minimum.

Step 1: Check your contract to see your holiday entitlement and whether it includes any enhanced holiday pay.  
Step 2: Calculate your holiday pay based on the hours you work, ensuring it is proportional to full-time entitlement.  
Step 3: If you have questions or concerns, contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/checking-holiday-entitlement  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics

question: Can my employer pay me instead of giving me holiday?

sources.title: Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, workers have a legal right to paid annual leave, and employers must provide this leave rather than simply paying you in lieu of holiday while you are still employed. However, there are some exceptions. For example, if your employment ends and you have unused holiday entitlement, your employer must pay you for this untaken holiday in your final pay. This is known as "holiday pay in lieu."

Employers cannot include holiday pay as part of your hourly rate (known as "rolled-up holiday pay") for most workers, especially if your leave year started after 31 March 2024. If you have questions about how holiday pay is calculated or your entitlement, you can contact Acas for guidance.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and holiday entitlement.
Step 2: If your employer is not allowing you to take holiday but wants to pay you instead, clarify whether your employment is ending or if this is during your employment.
Step 3: If your employment is ending, confirm how much holiday pay you are owed.
Step 4: Contact Acas or Citizens Advice if you need further help or if you believe your rights are not being met.

Sources used:
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job

question: Is rolled-up holiday pay legal in the UK?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Rolled-up holiday pay, where holiday pay is included in the hourly rate rather than paid separately during holiday, is generally not allowed for most workers in the UK. However, employers can use rolled-up holiday pay for irregular-hours and part-year workers, but only if their leave year began before 1 April 2024. For leave years starting on or after 1 April 2024, rolled-up holiday pay is not permitted.

If you have irregular hours or part-year work, your employer may still use rolled-up holiday pay if your leave year started before 31 March 2024. For other workers, holiday pay must be paid separately when you take your holiday. If you have questions about your specific situation, you can contact Acas for guidance.

Step 1: Check when your leave year started (before or after 1 April 2024).  
Step 2: Confirm if your work pattern is irregular-hours or part-year.  
Step 3: If unsure or if you believe your holiday pay is not being calculated correctly, contact Acas for advice.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights/holiday-pay-the-basics  
- https://www.gov.uk/holiday-entitlement-rights

question: PAY / WAGES / DEDUCTIONS / MINIMUM WAGE (13)

sources.title: What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage
If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers must pay workers at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage depending on their age and status. For example, from 1 April 2026, the National Living Wage for those aged 21 or over is £12.71 per hour. Apprentices and younger workers have different minimum rates. Employers must provide payslips showing how pay is calculated, and they can only make deductions from wages in limited, lawful circumstances.

If you believe your employer has underpaid you or made unlawful deductions, you should first try to resolve the issue by talking to them and showing any evidence you have. If this does not work, you can raise a formal grievance. If the problem persists, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages, but this must be done within three months minus one day from the date the wages were due. Alternatively, you can take a claim to a small claims court. It is important to check your employment status and the minimum wage rates applicable to you.

Step 1: Check your pay against the current minimum wage rates for your age and status.  
Step 2: Gather evidence such as payslips, contracts, and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 3: Talk to your employer to try and resolve the issue informally.  
Step 4: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance with your employer.  
Step 5: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal or small claims court if necessary, within the time limits.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/pay

question: My employer has not paid my wages on payday. What should I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has not paid your wages on the agreed payday, you should first talk to them to find out why. It may be a mistake that they can quickly fix by paying you what you are owed straight away. If talking to your employer does not resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance with them if they have a grievance procedure. Keep any evidence you have, such as payslips or contracts, to support your claim.

If the problem continues and your employer still does not pay you, you may be able to take legal action. You can make a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages, but you must do this within 3 months minus one day from the date you should have been paid. If you miss this deadline, you might be able to claim unpaid wages through a small claims court instead. It is usually best to try to resolve the issue without legal action if you plan to continue working for your employer.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department about the missing wages.  
Step 2: If unresolved, raise a formal grievance if possible.  
Step 3: Consider seeking advice from a trade union representative or an adviser.  
Step 4: If necessary, make a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limit or use the small claims court.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: My employer deducted money from my wages without explaining why.

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can only deduct money from your wages if it is allowed by your contract or if you have agreed in writing. For example, deductions can be made for damaged property, missing money from the till, or the cost of a uniform, but only if these are specified in your contract or you have given written consent. Additionally, if deductions are for missing money or damaged stock, your employer must follow extra rules: they can only deduct up to 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, must start deductions within 12 months of noticing the issue, and must provide you with written details of each deduction.

If your employer has deducted money without explaining why or without your agreement, you should first check your contract and any written agreements to see if such deductions are allowed. If you are unsure or believe the deduction is unfair, you should seek advice from an adviser or a relevant organisation.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Ask your employer to explain the deduction and provide written details.  
Step 3: If you disagree or don’t receive an explanation, seek advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: I think I am being paid below minimum wage. What can I do?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you think you are being paid below the National Minimum Wage (NMW) or National Living Wage (NLW), you should first check the current rates for your age group to confirm. Employers must pay at least the minimum wage for your age or apprenticeship status. If your pay is below this, your employer may be breaking the law. You can talk to your employer or payroll department to raise the issue and ask for the correct pay. If you are a member of a trade union, you can also ask your representative for help.

If talking to your employer does not resolve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance with them. If the issue remains unresolved, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. Keep any evidence you have, such as payslips or your employment contract, to support your claim. Remember, the deadline to start an employment tribunal claim is usually 3 months minus one day from when you should have been paid.

Step 1: Check the current minimum wage rates for your age or apprenticeship status.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer or payroll department about your pay concerns.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if the issue is not resolved informally.  
Step 4: Consider legal action such as an employment tribunal claim if necessary, keeping track of deadlines.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: What can I do if my employer pays me late every month?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer pays you late every month, you should first talk to them to find out why your pay is delayed and explain how it affects you. It’s important to keep a record of when you were supposed to be paid and when you actually received your wages. If your employer acknowledges the mistake, ask them to pay you as soon as possible and on time in the future.

If talking to your employer doesn’t resolve the issue, you can raise a formal grievance using your employer’s grievance procedure if they have one. Continuing to work while trying to resolve the problem is usually advisable, as refusing to work might breach your contract. If the problem persists and you are not paid what you are owed, you may be able to take legal action, such as making a claim to an employment tribunal for unlawful deduction of wages. However, this should be a last resort, and there are strict time limits for making such claims (usually within 3 months minus one day from the date the payment was due).

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department to understand the reason for late payments.  
Step 2: Keep records of pay dates and amounts.  
Step 3: Raise a formal grievance if necessary.  
Step 4: Consider legal action if the issue is not resolved, keeping in mind the time limits for claims.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Can my employer make deductions from my wages?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can make deductions from your wages only if it is allowed by your contract or you have agreed in writing. Common reasons for deductions include overpayments that you need to pay back, damaged property, missing money from the till, or the cost of a uniform. However, there are rules your employer must follow, especially for deductions related to missing money or damaged stock: they must have a written agreement, not deduct more than 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, start deductions within 12 months of noticing the issue, and provide you with written details of each deduction.

If you think your employer has made an unlawful deduction, you should first check your contract and any agreements you have. If you disagree with the deduction, you should talk to your employer or seek advice from an adviser. If the deduction brings your pay below the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage, this could be illegal.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements about deductions.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer to clarify or dispute the deduction.  
Step 3: Seek advice from a trade union representative or an employment adviser if needed.  
Step 4: If unresolved, consider raising a formal grievance or taking further action.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: My employer has not paid me for overtime. Is that legal?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, whether your employer must pay you for overtime depends on your contract and the terms agreed. There is no automatic legal right to overtime pay unless your contract or workplace policy states otherwise. However, your employer must pay you at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for all hours worked, including overtime. If your total pay divided by the total hours worked (including overtime) falls below the minimum wage, your employer is not paying you legally.

If you believe you are owed pay for overtime, check your contract and payslips to see what you agreed about overtime pay. If your employer has not paid you what you are owed, you should raise the issue with them first. If this does not resolve the problem, you may be able to make a claim for unpaid wages through an employment tribunal or small claims court, but there are time limits for doing this.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and payslips to confirm your entitlement to overtime pay.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer or payroll department to raise the issue.  
Step 3: If unresolved, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified adviser about making a claim for unpaid wages.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: What should be included on my payslip?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, your payslip should clearly show your pay and any deductions made from it. This includes your gross pay (the total amount before deductions), the amount of tax and National Insurance deducted, and any other deductions such as pension contributions or student loan repayments. It should also show your net pay (the amount you take home after deductions). Your payslip should be provided on or before your payday.

Payslips help you check that you have been paid correctly and understand how your pay is calculated. If you think there is a mistake or an unlawful deduction, you should first check your contract and any written agreements. If you are unsure, you can seek advice from an employment adviser.

Step 1: Check your payslip each payday to ensure it shows your gross pay, all deductions, and net pay.  
Step 2: Compare the payslip details with your contract or agreement to confirm deductions are lawful.  
Step 3: If you spot any errors or deductions you do not agree with, raise the issue with your employer or payroll department.  
Step 4: If the issue is not resolved, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/pay  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Can my employer withhold my final pay when I leave?

sources.title: Why final pay is different – Final pay when someone leaves a job
If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer should pay your final pay on the date they normally pay you, even if you leave partway through a pay period. This final pay may include outstanding wages, holiday pay, redundancy pay, or deductions for things like training courses. Employers must clearly explain how your final pay is calculated, and your payslip should show any payments or deductions. If you do not receive your final payslip or final pay on time, you should contact your employer to ask for clarification.

If your employer withholds your final pay without a valid reason, you can raise the issue with them directly. If this does not resolve the problem, you may be able to make a claim for unpaid wages through an employment tribunal or small claims court, but there are strict time limits for doing so (usually within 3 months minus a day from when you should have been paid). It is advisable to seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice if you face difficulties.

Step 1: Check your final payslip and payment date with your employer.  
Step 2: Ask your employer for an explanation if your final pay is missing or incorrect.  
Step 3: Contact ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance if the issue is not resolved.  
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal or small claims court if necessary, keeping in mind the time limits.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/final-pay-when-someone-leaves-a-job  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: What can I do if my employer owes me commission?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer owes you commission, this is considered part of your wages, and you have the right to be paid what you are owed according to your employment contract. First, you should try to resolve the issue by talking to your employer and showing any evidence you have, such as your contract or records of sales that should have earned you commission. If this does not work, check if your employer has a formal grievance procedure you can use to raise a complaint.

If your employer still refuses to pay, you may be able to take legal action. You can make a claim for unpaid wages through an employment tribunal or, if the tribunal deadline has passed, through a small claims court. The deadline for starting an employment tribunal claim is usually 3 months minus a day from when the payment was due. Keep in mind that making a claim should be a last resort, especially if you want to continue working for your employer.

Step 1: Gather evidence such as your contract and records of commission owed.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer to try and resolve the issue informally.  
Step 3: Use your employer’s grievance procedure if available.  
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the deadline or a small claims court if the deadline has passed.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Is it legal for my employer to charge me for training?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you
What is the minimum wage – National Minimum Wage

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Your employer can require you to do training, but they cannot use mandatory training as a reason not to pay you at least the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for the time spent training. Even if your contract says you won’t be paid for mandatory training, your average hourly pay must still meet the legal minimum wage requirements. If you are being charged or deducted money for training, this could be an unlawful deduction from your wages.

If you believe your employer is not paying you correctly for training time or is charging you unfairly, you should check your employment contract and any company policies related to training and pay. You may also want to gather evidence such as payslips and contracts to support your case.

Step 1: Review your employment contract and any training policies.  
Step 2: Check if your pay meets or exceeds the National Minimum Wage or National Living Wage for training hours.  
Step 3: If you think you are owed pay or have been unfairly charged, contact HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) or Citizens Advice for guidance on how to proceed.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/national-minimum-wage-entitlement

question: My employer says they overpaid me and wants the money back. What are my rights?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer says they overpaid you and wants the money back, they can deduct the overpaid amount from your wages, but only if you have agreed to this in writing or if your contract allows it. They must follow certain rules when making deductions for overpayments, especially if the deduction relates to missing money or damaged stock. For example, they cannot deduct more than 10% of your pay before tax on each payday, must start deductions within 12 months of noticing the overpayment, and must provide you with written details of each deduction.

If you do not agree that you were overpaid or cannot afford the deductions, you should explain why to your employer and ask them to pay you what you believe you are owed. You can also request smaller deductions spread over a longer period if you are struggling to repay the amount. If you are unsure about your rights or how to handle the situation, it is advisable to seek advice from an employment adviser.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and any written agreements to see if deductions for overpayments are allowed.  
Step 2: Talk to your employer to clarify the overpayment and discuss repayment options.  
Step 3: If you disagree or need help, contact an adviser for guidance.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: Can my employer change my pay without telling me?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, your employer should not change your pay without informing you. Your pay and any changes to it are usually set out in your contract or written statement of employment. If your employer wants to change your pay, they should discuss this with you first and get your agreement. If they make changes without telling you or without your consent, this could be a breach of your contract.

If your employer has changed your pay without informing you, you should check your contract and payslips carefully. You can talk to your employer or payroll department to clarify the situation. If you believe your pay has been unlawfully deducted or reduced, you can seek advice and potentially take legal action to recover the correct amount.

Step 1: Check your employment contract and payslips for details about your pay.  
Step 2: Speak to your employer or payroll department to ask about any changes.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: What should I do if I have not been paid for work I have already done?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer hasn’t paid you for work you have already done, you should first talk to them to find out why. Check your payslip if you have one to understand the issue better. If it was a mistake, ask your employer to pay you straight away and not wait until your next pay day. If talking to your employer doesn’t solve the problem, you can raise a formal grievance if your employer has a grievance procedure.

If the issue remains unresolved, you may consider taking legal action. You can make a claim to an employment tribunal for unpaid wages or unlawful deductions, but you must do this within 3 months minus one day from when you should have been paid. If you miss this deadline, you might still be able to claim through a small claims court. It’s usually best to try to resolve the issue directly with your employer before taking legal steps.

Step 1: Talk to your employer or payroll department to understand why you haven’t been paid.  
Step 2: If necessary, raise a formal grievance following your employer’s procedure.  
Step 3: If unresolved, consider legal action by contacting an employment tribunal or small claims court before the deadline.

Sources used:  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/  
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: DISMISSAL (13)
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Dismissal happens when an employer ends an employee’s contract, which is often called being sacked or fired. For a dismissal to be fair, the employer must have a valid reason, act reasonably, and follow a fair and consistent procedure, usually in line with the Acas Code of Practice. The employer should investigate the situation fully before dismissing someone and treat all employees equally in similar circumstances.

If you are dismissed, your employer must tell you why you have been dismissed, when your contract ends, your notice period, and your right to appeal. You have the right to ask for a written statement of the reasons for dismissal if you have been employed for at least two years (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012). Employers must provide this within 14 days of your request. Special rules apply if you are dismissed while on maternity leave, where the employer must give you the reasons in writing regardless of your length of service.

If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you can appeal the decision internally. If the employer does not follow a fair procedure, you may be able to bring a claim for unfair dismissal to an employment tribunal. However, if you successfully appeal and get your job back, this is called a "vanishing dismissal," and you cannot make an unfair dismissal claim. Settlement agreements can also be used to end employment by mutual consent, but signing one usually means you cannot bring an unfair dismissal claim.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written explanation of your dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 2: Consider appealing the dismissal if you think it was unfair.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice if you want to understand your rights or consider making a claim.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals  
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: I was dismissed without a meeting. Is that unfair dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Being dismissed without a meeting can be a sign that your employer did not follow a fair procedure. For a dismissal to be fair, your employer should have a valid reason and follow a fair and reasonable process, which usually includes investigating the situation and giving you a chance to respond, often through a meeting. If you were dismissed without any meeting or opportunity to explain your side, this could mean the procedure was unfair.

However, whether the dismissal is legally unfair depends on other factors too, such as how long you have worked there and the reason for dismissal. If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you can appeal the decision with your employer. If that does not resolve the issue, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Ask your employer for the reasons for your dismissal in writing if you have been employed for at least 2 years or are on maternity leave.  
Step 2: Consider appealing the dismissal with your employer, explaining your concerns about the lack of a meeting.  
Step 3: If the appeal does not resolve the issue, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a claim for unfair dismissal.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals  
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: My employer dismissed me and refused to let me appeal.
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has dismissed you and refused to allow you to appeal, this could be a concern because employers are expected to follow a fair and reasonable procedure when dismissing someone. According to UK employment law, a fair dismissal process usually includes giving you the chance to appeal the decision. Not allowing an appeal might be seen as not following a fair procedure, which could be relevant if you decide to claim unfair dismissal.

You have the right to be told why you were dismissed, when your employment ends, and your right to appeal. If you have been employed for at least two years, you can ask your employer for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal, and they must provide it within 14 days. If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you may want to seek advice on your options, including making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Ask your employer in writing for the reasons for your dismissal and whether you can appeal.  
Step 2: If your employer refuses to allow an appeal or you believe the dismissal was unfair, consider contacting ACAS or Citizens Advice for guidance.  
Step 3: If necessary, you may explore making a claim for unfair dismissal, but note there are time limits for doing so.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals  
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: Can I be dismissed without notice?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, your employer usually must give you notice before dismissing you. The notice period should be at least the length stated in your contract or the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer. However, there are some situations where your employer can dismiss you immediately without notice, such as in cases of gross misconduct like violence.

If you are dismissed, your employer must have a valid reason and act reasonably in the circumstances. They should also follow a fair procedure before dismissing you. If you have been dismissed, you have the right to ask for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal, provided you have worked for your employer for at least two years (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012). Certain employees, such as those on statutory maternity leave, have additional rights to receive this statement regardless of length of service.

Step 1: Check your employment contract for your notice period.  
Step 2: Consider if your dismissal was for a serious reason that might justify immediate dismissal.  
Step 3: Ask your employer for a written statement of reasons if you have the right to do so.  
Step 4: If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you may want to seek advice on appealing or making a claim.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: What is constructive dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Constructive dismissal happens when an employee resigns because their employer has behaved in a way that seriously breaches their contract, making it impossible for the employee to continue working there. This could include things like a significant change to job duties, not paying wages, or creating a hostile work environment. The employee must leave the job because of the employer’s conduct, not for other reasons.

If an employee believes they have been constructively dismissed, they may be able to claim unfair dismissal at an employment tribunal. It is important that the employee can show the employer’s behaviour was a fundamental breach of contract and that they resigned soon after the breach occurred.

Step 1: Consider if the employer’s actions amount to a serious breach of contract.  
Step 2: If so, resign promptly and clearly state that you are resigning because of the employer’s conduct.  
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about making a claim for unfair dismissal.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: How long do I need to work somewhere to claim unfair dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

To claim unfair dismissal in the UK, you generally need to have worked for your employer for at least 2 years. This means you must have the legal status of an employee and have been continuously employed for two years before the dismissal. If you meet this requirement, you can make a claim to an employment tribunal if you believe your dismissal was unfair.

If you have been employed for less than 2 years, you usually cannot claim unfair dismissal, although there are some exceptions, such as dismissal related to discrimination or whistleblowing. It is important that your employer follows a fair and reasonable procedure when dismissing you, including giving you the reasons for dismissal and the right to appeal.

Step 1: Check how long you have been employed and your employee status.  
Step 2: If you have worked for 2 years or more, consider whether your dismissal was fair and if the employer followed a proper procedure.  
Step 3: If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you can appeal internally or make a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limits.  

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: My employer says I was dismissed for poor performance. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer says you were dismissed for poor performance, they must have a valid reason and act reasonably in the circumstances. This means they should have investigated the situation fully and followed a fair procedure before dismissing you. They must also be consistent, not dismissing you for something they have allowed other employees to do. If you have been employed for at least 2 years (or 1 year if you started before 6 April 2012), you have the right to ask your employer for a written statement explaining the reasons for your dismissal, which they must provide within 14 days.

Your employer should also give you notice according to your contract or the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer. If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you can appeal the decision internally. If the appeal is unsuccessful, you may be able to make a claim for unfair dismissal to an employment tribunal, but you should consider this carefully and may want to seek advice before proceeding.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you have the right to do so.  
Step 2: Check if your employer followed a fair procedure and gave you proper notice.  
Step 3: Consider appealing the dismissal with your employer.  
Step 4: If you still believe the dismissal was unfair, seek advice about making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: Can I be dismissed while on sick leave?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Yes, you can be dismissed while on sick leave, but your employer must have a valid reason for doing so and must act reasonably in the circumstances. They should have investigated the situation fully before dismissing you. Employers must also be consistent in their treatment of employees and cannot dismiss you for reasons that they allow other employees to do without consequence. If you are dismissed, your employer must give you the appropriate notice period as stated in your contract or the statutory minimum notice period, whichever is longer.

If you have been dismissed while on Statutory Maternity Leave, your employer must provide you with a written statement explaining the reasons for dismissal, regardless of how long you have worked for them. For other types of sick leave, you have the right to ask for a written statement of reasons if you have completed at least two years’ service (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012).

Step 1: Check your employment contract and length of service to understand your rights regarding notice and written reasons for dismissal.  
Step 2: If you are dismissed, request a written statement of the reasons for your dismissal if you are eligible.  
Step 3: If you believe your dismissal was unfair or not handled properly, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/holiday-sickness-leave

question: What should happen in a fair disciplinary process?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

A fair disciplinary process in the UK should follow a clear and reasonable procedure to ensure the employee is treated fairly. The employer must have a valid reason for the disciplinary action and make decisions that are balanced, consistent, and as fair as possible. The process usually involves informing the employee of the issues, allowing them to respond, and giving them a chance to improve or appeal any decisions made.

Employers are encouraged to follow the Acas Code of Practice on disciplinary and grievance procedures, which outlines steps such as investigating the matter properly, holding a disciplinary meeting, and providing the employee with the right to appeal. Confidentiality should be maintained throughout the process, and any outcome should be communicated clearly, ideally in writing.

Step 1: Employer investigates the issue thoroughly.  
Step 2: Employee is informed of the concerns and invited to a disciplinary meeting.  
Step 3: Disciplinary meeting is held, allowing the employee to respond.  
Step 4: Employer makes a decision and informs the employee, providing reasons and any right to appeal.  
Step 5: Employee can appeal the decision if they believe it is unfair.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: Can I be made redundant without consultation?

sources.title: If your employer hasn’t paid you what they owe you

answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, employers are generally expected to consult with employees before making them redundant. Consultation is important because it allows employees to understand the reasons for redundancy, explore alternatives, and discuss the selection process. For larger redundancies (usually 20 or more employees), there is a legal requirement for collective consultation with employee representatives or trade unions. For smaller numbers, individual consultation is still considered best practice.

If an employer makes someone redundant without any consultation, this could be unfair dismissal unless there are exceptional circumstances. It is advisable to raise concerns with the employer or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice if you believe your redundancy was handled improperly.

Step 1: Check if your employer followed a consultation process.
Step 2: If not, raise the issue with your employer or HR department.
Step 3: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for further support.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals
- https://www.citizensadvice.org.uk/work/pay/problems-getting-paid/

question: What is the difference between redundancy and dismissal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Dismissal is when an employer ends an employee's contract, which is often called being sacked or fired. It can happen for various reasons, such as poor performance, misconduct, or other valid reasons. Employers must follow a fair and reasonable procedure when dismissing someone, including giving reasons for the dismissal and allowing the employee to appeal.

Redundancy is a specific type of dismissal that happens when an employer needs to reduce their workforce. This could be because the job is no longer needed, the business is closing, or the employer needs fewer employees to do the work. Redundancy is different from other dismissals because it is not about the employee's conduct or performance but about the role itself being eliminated.

Step 1: Understand that dismissal is a broad term for ending employment, while redundancy is a specific reason related to the job no longer existing.  
Step 2: Check if the employer has followed a fair procedure and given proper notice and reasons.  
Step 3: If you think the dismissal or redundancy was unfair, you can appeal or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: My employer dismissed me for raising a complaint. Is this unfair?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer dismissed you because you raised a complaint, this could potentially be unfair dismissal, especially if the complaint was made in good faith and related to a workplace issue. Employers must have a valid reason for dismissal and follow a fair and reasonable procedure. Dismissing someone for raising a complaint may be seen as victimisation or retaliation, which is generally not allowed.

You have the right to appeal the dismissal and your employer should follow a fair procedure during this process. If you believe the dismissal was unfair, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal. It is important to act quickly, as there are time limits for making claims.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written explanation of the reasons for your dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 2: Consider appealing the dismissal through your employer’s internal procedures.  
Step 3: If you still believe the dismissal was unfair, seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: Can I challenge my dismissal at a tribunal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

You can challenge your dismissal at an employment tribunal if you believe it was unfair. For a dismissal to be fair, your employer must have a valid reason, act reasonably, and follow a fair procedure. This includes investigating the situation fully and being consistent in how they treat employees. If you have been employed for at least two years (or one year if you started before 6 April 2012), you have the right to request a written statement from your employer explaining the reasons for your dismissal, which they must provide within 14 days.

If you think your dismissal was unfair, you should first consider appealing the decision with your employer. If the appeal is unsuccessful, you may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal. However, if you accept a settlement agreement, you usually give up the right to make an unfair dismissal claim. It is important to act quickly because there are strict time limits for making tribunal claims.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written statement of reasons for dismissal if you haven’t already received one.  
Step 2: Consider appealing the dismissal internally with your employer.  
Step 3: If the appeal fails, seek advice and consider making a claim to an employment tribunal within the time limit.  

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals  
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal

question: What should I do if I believe my dismissal was unfair?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you believe your dismissal was unfair, you should first check whether your employer followed a fair and reasonable procedure before dismissing you. Employers must have a valid reason for dismissal and should have given you clear reasons for why you were dismissed, ideally in writing. You have the right to appeal the dismissal within your workplace, and your employer should allow you to do this through a fair process.

If you appeal and your dismissal is overturned and you get your job back, this is called a "vanishing dismissal," meaning you cannot then make a claim for unfair dismissal to an employment tribunal. However, if you are still dismissed unfairly after a fair procedure, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal. It is important to act quickly, as there are strict time limits for making claims.

Step 1: Ask your employer for a written explanation of the reasons for your dismissal if you have not already received one.  
Step 2: Use your employer’s appeal process to challenge the dismissal.  
Step 3: If the appeal is unsuccessful and you still believe the dismissal was unfair, consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: DISCRIMINATION / HARASSMENT / VICTIMISATION (12)
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is unlawful for employers or anyone at work to discriminate against someone because of protected characteristics such as age, gender reassignment, pregnancy, disability, race, religion, sex, or sexual orientation. Discrimination can be direct (treating someone less favourably) or indirect (applying a policy that disadvantages a group). Harassment and victimisation related to these characteristics are also prohibited. Employers have a responsibility to prevent discrimination and bullying, take complaints seriously, and handle them properly.

If you experience or witness discrimination, harassment, or victimisation at work, you can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer. Employers should follow a fair procedure to investigate and resolve complaints. If the issue is not resolved, you may have the right to bring a claim to an employment tribunal. Employers should also respect confidentiality when dealing with such matters.

Step 1: Identify if the treatment you or someone else has experienced relates to a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010.  
Step 2: Report the issue to your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint procedure.  
Step 3: If the employer does not resolve the issue, consider seeking advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about taking further action, including a tribunal claim.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment  
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: I was treated unfairly at work because of my religion. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is illegal for an employer or anyone at work to discriminate against you because of your religion or belief. This protection covers all aspects of your employment, including hiring, pay, promotion, and dismissal. You are also protected if you are harassed or victimised because of your religion, or if you have supported someone else’s claim related to discrimination.

If you believe you have been treated unfairly due to your religion, you can raise the issue with your employer, who should take your complaint seriously and follow a fair procedure to address it. Employers are required to prevent discrimination and bullying at work. If the problem is not resolved internally, you may be able to make a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Raise your concerns informally with your employer or HR department.  
Step 2: If informal discussions do not resolve the issue, use your employer’s formal grievance procedure.  
Step 3: Seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice for support and guidance on next steps.  
Step 4: Consider making a claim to an employment tribunal if the issue remains unresolved.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: My manager is harassing me. Is this discrimination or harassment?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Harassment at work is unwanted behaviour linked to a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010, such as age, sex, race, disability, religion, or sexual orientation. It can include offensive jokes, insults, or other behaviour that creates a hostile or intimidating work environment. Harassment is a form of discrimination and is unlawful. Bullying, while not always linked to a protected characteristic, is also unacceptable and employers should take complaints seriously.

If your manager is harassing you, this could be discrimination if it relates to one of the protected characteristics. You have the right to raise this with your employer, who must take steps to prevent harassment and deal with complaints properly. You can choose to take informal or formal action depending on your situation.

Step 1: Document the incidents of harassment, noting dates, times, and what was said or done.  
Step 2: Report the behaviour to your employer or HR department, following your workplace’s complaints procedure.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about your next steps.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment  
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: What is workplace discrimination under the Equality Act 2010?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Workplace discrimination under the Equality Act 2010 means treating someone unfairly or less favorably because they have a protected characteristic. Protected characteristics include age, gender reassignment, being married or in a civil partnership, pregnancy or maternity, disability, race (including colour, nationality, ethnic or national origin), religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation. Discrimination can happen in various ways, such as direct discrimination (treating someone worse because of a protected characteristic), indirect discrimination (applying a rule that disadvantages people with a protected characteristic), harassment, or victimisation.

The law protects employees from discrimination at work, and employers have a duty to prevent it and handle complaints seriously. You are also protected if you are associated with someone who has a protected characteristic or if you have complained about discrimination or supported someone else’s claim.

Step 1: Identify if the treatment you or someone else has experienced relates to a protected characteristic.  
Step 2: Report the discrimination to your employer, either informally or formally, so they can take appropriate action.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, you may seek further advice from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: Can I be treated differently because of my disability?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, it is unlawful to treat someone less favourably because of a disability. Disability is one of the protected characteristics, meaning you are legally protected from discrimination at work and in other areas such as education and public services. Employers must make reasonable adjustments to accommodate disabled employees to ensure they are not at a disadvantage compared to others.

If you believe you have been treated unfairly or discriminated against because of your disability, you can raise this with your employer or seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice. Employers are also encouraged to take positive action to help people with disabilities if they face particular needs or disadvantages.

Step 1: Identify if the treatment you experienced relates to your disability.  
Step 2: Raise the issue informally with your employer or HR department if possible.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, consider making a formal complaint or seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: What is victimisation at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Victimisation at work happens when an employer or colleague treats you badly because you have made a complaint about discrimination, supported someone else's discrimination claim, or raised a concern about discrimination. It is a form of unfair treatment that is prohibited under the Equality Act 2010. Victimisation can include things like being ignored, given less work, or being unfairly disciplined because of your involvement in a discrimination issue.

If you believe you are being victimised at work, you can raise the issue informally with your employer or follow a formal complaint procedure. Employers have a responsibility to prevent victimisation and handle complaints seriously to ensure a safe and fair workplace.

Step 1: Keep a record of any incidents of victimisation.
Step 2: Report the behaviour to your employer, either informally or through a formal grievance.
Step 3: Seek advice from organisations like ACAS or Citizens Advice if needed.

Sources used:
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: My employer ignored my complaint about discrimination. What can I do?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If your employer has ignored your complaint about discrimination, you have several options. First, you can raise the issue informally by speaking to your employer or HR department again, making sure to keep a record of your communications. If this does not resolve the problem, you can follow your employer’s formal grievance procedure to make a formal complaint. Employers have a legal duty under the Equality Act 2010 to take discrimination complaints seriously and to prevent discrimination at work.

If your employer still does not address your complaint, you may be able to bring a claim to an employment tribunal for discrimination. It is advisable to seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice, who can guide you on the next steps and help you understand your rights and options.

Step 1: Try to resolve the issue informally by discussing it with your employer or HR.  
Step 2: If informal talks fail, raise a formal grievance using your employer’s grievance procedure.  
Step 3: If the grievance is ignored or unresolved, seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about making a tribunal claim.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment  
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: Is bullying at work illegal?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Bullying at work is not a specific criminal offence, but it can be considered unlawful if it amounts to harassment or discrimination under the Equality Act 2010. Employers have a legal duty to prevent bullying and harassment and to take complaints seriously. If bullying is related to a protected characteristic such as age, race, sex, disability, or other grounds covered by the Equality Act, it may be unlawful discrimination.

If you experience or witness bullying, you can raise the issue with your employer informally or formally. Employers should have policies and procedures in place to handle bullying and discrimination complaints and are expected to investigate and address such issues properly.

Step 1: Check your employer’s bullying and harassment policy.  
Step 2: Report the bullying to your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice about next steps, including potential legal claims.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: What counts as harassment at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Harassment at work is unwanted behaviour linked to a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010, which violates a person's dignity or creates an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment. This can include things like offensive jokes, name-calling, bullying, physical abuse, or unwanted physical contact. Harassment can be direct or indirect and may be carried out by employers, colleagues, or even third parties.

Employers have a legal duty to prevent harassment and must take complaints seriously, investigating and addressing them appropriately. If you experience or witness harassment, you can raise the issue informally or formally with your employer, who should have procedures in place to handle such complaints.

Step 1: Identify the behaviour and whether it relates to a protected characteristic (such as age, sex, race, disability, etc.).  
Step 2: Report the harassment to your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint.  
Step 3: If the employer does not resolve the issue, you may seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice for further steps.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment  
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights

question: Can my employer refuse reasonable adjustments for my disability?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, specifically the Equality Act 2010, employers have a legal duty to make reasonable adjustments for employees with disabilities. This means they must take reasonable steps to remove or reduce any disadvantages caused by the disability in the workplace. However, an employer can refuse to make adjustments if they can show that doing so would cause them a disproportionate burden or significant difficulty, considering factors like cost and practicality.

If you believe your employer has unreasonably refused to make adjustments, you can raise the issue with them informally or through a formal grievance procedure. If the problem is not resolved, you may have grounds to make a discrimination claim under the Equality Act 2010.

Step 1: Inform your employer about your disability and the adjustments you need.  
Step 2: Provide any medical evidence if requested to support your request.  
Step 3: If your employer refuses, ask for a clear explanation in writing.  
Step 4: Consider raising a formal grievance if necessary.  
Step 5: Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor if the issue remains unresolved.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: I was treated unfairly because of my race. What are my rights?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

Under UK law, it is illegal to be treated unfairly or discriminated against because of your race. Race is a protected characteristic under the Equality Act 2010, which means you are protected from discrimination at work and in other areas such as education and using public services. Discrimination can include direct discrimination, harassment, victimisation, or being treated less favourably because of your race or associated characteristics.

If you believe you have been discriminated against at work because of your race, you can raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal complaint or grievance procedure. Employers are required to take complaints seriously and take steps to prevent discrimination. You may also seek advice and support from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice.

Step 1: Document what happened and gather any evidence of the unfair treatment.  
Step 2: Raise the issue with your employer, either informally or through a formal grievance procedure.  
Step 3: If the issue is not resolved, consider seeking advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about your options, which may include making a claim to an employment tribunal.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment

question: Can I be dismissed for making a discrimination complaint?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

In the UK, it is generally unlawful for an employer to dismiss you because you have made a complaint about discrimination. This is considered a form of victimisation under the Equality Act 2010, which protects employees from being treated unfairly or dismissed for asserting their rights related to discrimination. If you believe you have been dismissed for making a discrimination complaint, this could be an unfair dismissal or a discriminatory dismissal, and you may have grounds to challenge it.

If you are dismissed, your employer must have a valid reason and act reasonably in the circumstances. They must also follow a fair procedure before dismissing you. If you think your dismissal is unfair or related to your discrimination complaint, you can appeal the decision and potentially bring a claim to an employment tribunal.

Step 1: Keep records of your discrimination complaint and any related communications.  
Step 2: If dismissed, ask your employer in writing for the reasons for your dismissal.  
Step 3: Consider appealing the dismissal through your employer’s internal procedures.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS, Citizens Advice, or a qualified solicitor about your situation and possible claims.

Sources used:  
- https://www.gov.uk/dismissal  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/dismissals

question: What should I do if I experience harassment at work?
answer:
Information only (not legal advice).

If you experience harassment at work, your employer has a legal duty under the Equality Act 2010 to prevent it and take your complaint seriously. You can choose to address the issue informally by speaking to your employer or a manager, or formally by making a written complaint through your employer’s grievance procedure. Employers should investigate your complaint and take appropriate action to stop the harassment.

It is important to keep a record of what happened, including dates, times, locations, and any witnesses. If you feel unable to raise the issue with your employer or if the problem continues, you may seek advice from organisations such as ACAS or Citizens Advice. They can guide you on your rights and possible next steps.

Step 1: Keep a detailed record of the harassment incidents.  
Step 2: Consider raising the issue informally with your employer or manager.  
Step 3: If needed, make a formal complaint using your employer’s grievance procedure.  
Step 4: Seek advice from ACAS or Citizens Advice if the issue is not resolved.

Sources used:  
- https://www.acas.org.uk/discrimination-bullying-and-harassment  
- https://www.gov.uk/discrimination-your-rights
